body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: YANG Data Model for Scheduled Attributes
Using off-path mechanisms for exposing Time-Variant Routing information
Applicability of TVR YANG Data Models
TVR off path exposure (was ALTO exposure)

For the node your model it depends on whether the topology changes are caused by the environment or caused by the cent centrally controlled. If the change is caused by the environment for example the links may be powered off due to the movement or sunlight changes or even weather changes. So in this case the node model is not applicable because these changes are uh originated by the node itself and uh if those changes are caused by the centralized control in this case the devices themselves they don't know when to adjust this attributes. So the topology your model is uh sorry this is node your model is applicable to deliver the schedules uh to the to the node to to indicate when when the ner to change the attributes. Okay. So the topology model uh has been already discussed in the last ITL meeting. Why we didn't change too much for this part and uh uh the result is the topology model is only applicable when the uh when the schedule is generated in a centralized manner and also executed in a centralized manner but on the different devices. All the schedule is generated in a centralized manner but executed in a distributed manner. Okay. And uh that's uh so the second question is how to encode your model. We have also we have discussed the question in last meeting but this time we nearly added the co conf as the optional protocols. So the conclusion is that the netcom would be the preferred pro protocol for large scale uh critical scheduling operations requiring validation and rollback mechanisms. uh and the rest conf would be the preferred protocol for smaller scale or isolated scheduling tasks and the cocon would be the preferred protocol for resource constraint IoT networks where s uh message bias is a priority okay for the time of secondization is the fundamental of ensuring that TVR mechanisms. It the schedule is scooter just need to undertake action at uh specific specified time points as indicated in the schedule. But for the topology schedule the uh the linking come up or not drawing to the topology uh should be have should not have any functional changes until the change is proven to be fully operational. However, if uh there is a link or node is to be removed from the topology, then the network should act before the anticipated change to root traffic around the expected topology change. Uh for the uh schedule recovery, it uh it means a node may lost it specific schedule date. This is maybe for example a a device restart then it it lost all his schedule data. So once the connection between the manager device and the manager device is established again then the managing device should redistribute all schedules that the start time is later than the current moment to the manager device. We also list two kind of error handling measures here. Uh they are the uh the schedule conflict uh the time parameters of schedule conflicts with each other. And I could see certainly two cases where that would be the case. One is a centralized scheduler has uh made an error or is making an update, in which case it it seems to me that the more recent schedule might be the one uh to go forward with and not to discard. Alternatively, if you uh if this was meant to handle cases of decentralized scheduling, then that might be out of scope for the error handling discussion. And I was just wondering uh which case you were thinking of when you were talking about error handling here. Uh while uh for the conflicts between different schedules uh it means it means if we if we have if we have uh two detailed schedule if uh uh for example one is start from uh for link and it start to power on from 10:00 And another is is uh another schedule says this link should be powered off at 10:00 then these two schedules are uh are conflict they both in in via model. This is from one controller but within within why your models the two schedules are conflict. >> Okay, that that might be something uh to clarify and and which of the schedules should be discarded and how to look at freshness. I do have one other comment, but let me leave the queue because I I see Joel is in the queue and then come back. >> Thank you. >> Okay. Thank you. Um uh you were talking about the need for synchronization of the clocks and yeah, there's clearly a need for the clocks to end up synchronized. But then I stopped and thought about what is it that needs to be synchronized and I realized you assert that it's that this controller providing the schedule needs to be synchronized with the node using it and I don't think that's the correct relationship. For example, if the node is getting a schedule to stop using a link because it's going to go away or come into existence because of physics like orbital rotation, then it's critical that the nodes clock be synchronized with the physics. That's then the right sync. In contrast, if the node is being told it will be able to use a link or not able to use a link because of management action, then it's that management station's clock that needs to be synced with the node's clock, which is not necessarily the same as the management station providing the schedule. So the the node needs to be synchronized with the underlying source of the scheduling information, but that isn't necessarily the thing that's providing the schedule to the node. And so I think sorry the problem is much more complicated than you paint it. >> Uh are I think you are right. Okay. I think we can we can uh consider more conflict uh complicated uh cases.
